items.title: Turmoil in the Middle East: Implications for Supply Chains
items.summary: The U.S.-Iran conflict has damaged productive infrastructure across the Middle East. An important implication is that supply-chain pressures are likely to linger, and we see some looming challenges for global growth and inflation. We offer analysis and consider the equity implications.

items.title: Global Commodities: Third-Quarter Outlook
items.summary: Our third-quarter commodities outlook focuses on recent geopolitical developments. Our base case sees sustained normalization in Strait of Hormuz flows; outside energy, we highlight upside risks in agriculture from El Niño. We also maintain bullish views on aluminum, copper and chocolate.

items.title: European Equity Strategy: UK Equity Implications of Burnham's Win
items.summary: A Burnham premiership for the UK would likely imply higher borrowing and potentially higher gilt yields. For equities, higher yields support FTSE 100 outperformance over the more rates-sensitive/domestically focused FTSE 250. We offer sector thoughts and further analysis in this note.

items.title: Japan Strategy: Tech-Led Bull Market Continues
items.summary: Japanese equities have continued rising, with TOPIX recently breaking above 4,000 and the Nikkei 225 topping 72,000, and we see the rally as staying within healthy bounds. We maintain our TOPIX target at 4,500 points while raising our Nikkei 225 target to 90,000.

items.title: Brazil Equity Strategy MVPs: Brazil Looks Increasingly Inexpensive
items.summary: Our Brazil MVP portfolio has outperformed the IBOV since mid-May. We note Brazil's 8.4x forward P/E represents one of the widest discounts to developed markets in recent memory, and see a number of reasons that the risk/reward for patient allocators increasingly looks skewed to the upside.

items.title: Taiwan Economics: We See GDP Momentum Accelerating
items.summary: We upgrade our 2026 real GDP forecast for Taiwan to 9.9%, driven by ultra-strong and broadening export momentum. Broadening macro resiliency could deliver higher inflation with some delay; we forecast CPI to rise to 2.2% in 2027 and so still expect rate hikes to resume in December.

items.title: Artificial Intelligence: Citi’s Inference Ahead — Open Vs. Closed
items.summary: The AI world’s focus is on the revelation that model access is subject to government fiat and the need to expand on-premise, open source, and non-U.S. model investment accordingly. This revelation will accelerate open-source adoption. We consider the implications further in this note.

items.title: European Economics: Will France's Economy Stay Underpressurized?
items.summary: France’s economy appears "underpressurized" compared with its peers, with domestic demand lackluster and lower inflation pressures. We explore what's behind this and argue France may have to continue going through a process of internal devaluation, which may take a while to run its course.

items.title: PULSE Monitor: A Hawkish Start
items.summary: Following new Fed Chair Warsh's first FOMC meeting, we see several potential policy paths to go down. We explore scenarios in this note, from oil futures and geopolitics to the labor market and inflation, and explain why we expect the rate discussion to persist through the summer months.

items.title: Global Macro Strategy: Kevin’s World — Home Alone Minus Guidance
items.summary: After months of waiting, we finally have a better sense of what a Kevin Warsh Fed meeting looks like. We explore our strategies in response, discuss why the U.S.-Iran MoU is meaningful and should have legs for further unwinds of oil beta trades, and also review the other G10 central banks.

items.title: Bank of England: Steady As She Goes
items.summary: As expected, the Bank of England voted to hold rates steady; while messaging was still on the hawkish end, policymakers are starting to lean against given the underlying weakness in the real economy. We continue to expect a hold this year, with the next cut coming in 2027's second quarter.

items.title: We're Upgrading Emirates Telecommunications Group to Buy
items.summary: We upgrade EAND to Buy and raise our target price to AED 23.0. EAND remains well positioned in its home market, with very strong market share. We offer further analysis in this note and explain why we see a clear pathway for enhanced shareholder returns.

items.title: Emerging Markets Strategy Weekly: Passing the Risk Baton
items.summary: Oil-related risks have subsided significantly and there's probably more room to go. Emerging Markets Asia should be the biggest beneficiary and there are still enough premia priced in, but we think the baton of risk factors will only get passed from oil to the Fed and El Niño.

items.title: European Rates Weekly: Green Light for Carry Trades?
items.summary: The ECB’s sticky hike premium will keep duration in a range, but vol should continue correcting lower, and we like receiving € 2s5s30s as a normalization trade. We also offer analysis of the UK's MPC meeting and Germany's third-quarter issuance update, and outline recommended strategies.

items.title: U.S. Economics: How Serious Are Fed Officials About Hiking Rates?
items.summary: 9 of 18 "dots" expecting rate hikes at the remaining four FOMC meetings this year were much more hawkish than we or markets had expected. But many of those same officials expect to be cutting rates again in 2027. In this note we unpack Chair Warsh's first meeting as head of the Fed.

items.title: We're Upgrading Albemarle to Buy
items.summary: We see ALB as a primary beneficiary of structural lithium demand given its high-quality assets, conversion network, and potential to allocate a steady capital program to high-return growth projects. We upgrade it to Buy as we think current valuations underappreciate its next leg of growth.

items.title: Assessing ELF's Current Challenges & Opportunities Ahead
items.summary: We take a Deep Dive into investor debates around e.l.f. Beauty. We recognize the challenges facing the core business — for which investors seek proof that management’s responses are working — but we think the concerns are largely reflected in the share price and maintain our Buy rating.

items.title: Global Industrials: Automation Survey Signals Encouraging Demand Outlook
items.summary: Our 7th annual automation survey of 200 manufacturing decision-makers points to encouraging demand momentum across automation spending despite modest macro-related concerns. Buy-rated ROK, EMR, HON and Siemens are our preferred automation-exposed names. We cover detailed responses herein.

items.title: U.S. Property & Casualty: M&A Activity Likely to Increase Across Sector
items.summary: We expect consolidation across the non-life insurance sector to increase. The three factors that accompanied prior periods of increased M&A activity are now present—below trend premium growth, above-average return on premium, and average valuations. We examine details and stocks herein.

items.title: European Electrical Equipment: Datacentre GW Growth Matters More Than Technology Mix
items.summary: While electrical equipment stocks are seen as key beneficiaries of datacentre growth, there's increasing focus on the relative growth outlooks between stocks. In this note, we show that overall growth in datacentre GW is more important than technology mix and outline our stock picks.

items.title: China Data Centers: Riding the AI Infrastructure Buildout
items.summary: In this cross-sector analysis covering China’s data centers and REITs, we delve into the convergence of AI capex, structural data center demand, and an emerging REIT capital recycling mechanism to highlight a compelling infrastructure investment thesis and the stocks that should benefit.

items.title: We're Launching Coverage of Figma with a Buy/High-Risk Rating
items.summary: Despite AI disruption of design and seat compression risks, we believe the market underestimates Figma's offsetting growth from seat mix upgrades, expanding non-designer personas, and rising AI-driven consumption. We're above-consensus on revenue estimates and see several catalysts ahead.

items.title: More Conviction on Buy-Rated Datadog Following DASH Conference
items.summary: Datadog's annual DASH customer conference featured a torrent of portfolio updates and expansion that we felt were materially up year over year. Key announcements and robust industry feedback reinforce our conviction in healthy 2H model upside. We raise our target price to $270 from $218.

items.title: U.S. Semiconductor Equipment: $250B Reasons to Stay Long
items.summary: We update our bull-case wafer fab equipment (WFE) revenue forecasts to $145B, $200B, and $250B for 2026, 2027, and 2028; align AMAT, LRCX, and KLAC estimates to our revised WFE model; and lift our price targets on those stocks as we roll forward our price targets based on 2028 earnings.

items.title: U.S. Equity Market Outlook: Iran Conflict Unwind and the Broadening Setup
items.summary: Markets are clearly encouraged that the current Iran negotiations will have a good chance of success. This is most evident in the price of WTI Crude, which has broken to its lowest levels since the early days of the conflict. Meanwhile, the "broadening" trade has already kicked in.

items.title: Equity Markets Positioning: U.S. Positioning Broadens as Cross-Regional Divergence Builds
items.summary: Global equity positioning remains constructive, but regional dynamics are increasingly divergent. In the U.S., positioning has broadened, but Nasdaq positioning risks remain. In Europe, conviction-driven longs return, and in Asia, a re-emergence of shorts tempers elevated positioning.

items.title: Global FX Strategy: EURGBP at Key Levels; Gilt Flows May Be the Key Driver
items.summary: We've had a bullish EURGBP bias – where recent indicators have been suggesting EURGBP should already be higher – yet price is again re-testing the bottom of the range around 0.86. We attribute this to overseas buying of gilts. The main question for GBP is whether such buying will continue.

items.title: Pricing Pass-Throughs Fuel Japan's Virtuous Cycle
items.summary: In today’s Japan, where a virtuous cycle of wages and prices is increasingly taking hold, companies have become much more proactive on price pass-throughs than during past energy shocks, fueling a shift to a dynamic in which rising costs boost nominal growth. We examine the implications.

items.title: China Economics: AI Supercycle Vs. Domestic Stagflation
items.summary: China's K-shaped economy appears to be playing out through the contrast between an AI supercycle and domestic stagflation. AI is driving industrial production and exports, with high-tech IP reaching a five-year high, but domestic demand is faltering. We offer further analysis in this note.

items.title: U.S. Biotech & Pharma: 2026 Halftime Report
items.summary: The Middle East conflict and the pervasive AI-led tech trade have meant a choppier-than-expected ride for Biopharma since early March. But fundamentals remain strong, with robust projected growth in earnings power and a forward P/E of ~17x highlighting potential undervalue to unlock.

items.title: Takeaways From Our India Defense Manufacturing Tour
items.summary: Following our tour of key companies and manufacturing facilities, we see India’s defense manufacturing sector as in a phase of structural scale-up and capability enhancement, with an intent to pivot toward integrated systems and proprietary offerings. See company-specific takeaways herein.

items.title: Global Gas and Power Insights: Further Downside?
items.summary: Despite the recent 15-20% selloff of TTF/JKM, we see further price downside amid de-escalation from the Mideast conflict; for U.S. Henry Hub, timing is important. We offer revised price forecasts and further analysis of what may lie ahead in this note.

items.title: Lone Dissenting Vote on Japan's Rate Hike Is a Dovish Signal
items.summary: Japan raised its policy rate to 1%, as expected, and decided to halt the reduction in JGB purchases from April 2027. As we explore in this note, we see the lone dissenting vote as an important dovish signal for the policy path. We continue to expect additional hikes in December and June.

items.title: Despite Dovish Murmurs, Australia Is Likely to Hike Again
items.summary: Communications following Australia's neutral to hawkish hold on rates at June's meeting highlighted upside risks to inflation and that further tightening hadn't been ruled out. Therefore, we continue to expect a 25-basis-point hike in August, though risks are tilted toward delayed hiking.
